original_abstract:
Electrochemical propylene epoxidation using water as an oxygen source offers a sustainable alternative to traditional industrial methods that rely on hazardous chlorine or peroxide reagents. In this study, we developed an oxidized palladium-platinum alloy catalyst (PdPtOx/C) capable of achieving a Faradaic efficiency of 66±5% for propylene oxide production at 50 mA/cm² under ambient conditions. Systematic characterization reveals that integrating platinum into the palladium oxide lattice stabilizes Pt(II) species, which significantly enhances catalytic activity and selectivity compared to pure metal oxides. 

Kinetic analysis, supported by multi-parameter modeling and probe substrate studies, suggests that epoxidation on PdPtOx/C proceeds through an electrophilic attack by metal-bound peroxo (OO*) intermediates. Furthermore, we demonstrate that this system is compatible with both organic-aqueous blends and fully aqueous electrolytes, the latter facilitated by the use of gas-diffusion electrodes to overcome mass-transport limitations. By establishing a correlation between alkene nucleophilicity and reaction rate, this work identifies electronic descriptors to guide further catalyst design. These findings provide a framework for selective, mediator-free electrochemical oxygen-atom transfer from water, advancing the potential for de-fossilized chemical manufacturing.
humanized_abstract:
This communication presents a sustainable, water-based electrochemical method of propylene oxidation, replacing the toxic and expensive chlorination and peroxidation processes currently used for manufacture of PO. We prepared an oxidized palladium-platinum alloy catalyst (PdPtOx/C) and found that it produced PO with Faradaic efficiency of 66±5% at current density of 50 mA/cm² at room temperature. By inclusion of platinum in palladium oxide lattice, we find that the catalyst is stabilized by the presence of platinum(II) which on a comparative study of catalysts showed enhanced catalytic performance in terms of yield and selectivity towards desired PO product over simple palladium and palladium oxide.

We report on kinetic analyses using multi-parameter modelling and probe substrate studies that reveal that the proposed catalytic cycle for the electro-epoxidation on PdPtOx/C is based on an electrophilic reaction based on metal bound peroxo species OO*. We also show that this catalyst can be used in liquid organic-carbon solutions as well as fully aqueous solutions, facilitated by the use of gas-diffusion electrodes to limit mass transport. The catalyst activity with a series of probe alkenes of differing nucleophilic activities shows a good correlation with the Hammett parameter, allowing us to define three electronic parameters which might be useful in the design of catalysts with tailored selectivity for mediator free or catalytic oxygen evolution from water. Our results open a route for the production of de-fossilized chemicals.

document.input:
